metadata.help: constant from d'Eon et al. 2011, converted to color via exp(-c)
constant from d'Eon et al. 2011, converted to color via exp(-c)
Surface bitangent vector, defaults to the current world-space bitangent vector.
Density of flake coverage (0 = no flakes, 1 = full coverage).
Surface normal; defaults to the current world-space normal.
Roughness of the flake normal distribution.
Size of individual flakes.
Surface tangent vector, defaults to the current world-space tangent vector.
The input 2d space. Default is the first texture coordinates.
Surface bitangent vector, defaults to the current world-space bitangent vector.
Density of flake coverage (0 = no flakes, 1 = full coverage).
Surface normal; defaults to the current world-space normal.
The input 3d space. Default is position in object-space.
Roughness of the flake normal distribution.
Size of individual flakes.
Surface tangent vector, defaults to the current world-space tangent vector.
Unit is degree.

Index of refraction (often denoted by eta), defining the amount reflected by the surface in the normal direction, and how the rays are bent by refraction. A separate value can be specified for each color channel, if desired.
metadata.label: IOR

metadata.help: Interior volume absorption color. It is defined as the negative logarithm of the extinction coefficient, with values between 0 and 1, such that the object itself and its shadows are tinted proportionally. If set to 1, there is no absorption, and if set to 0, the object will be completely opaque. Note that for single scattering to kick in, the value must be inferior to 1. This value will also act as coating color, when this node is used as top layer in a stack.
metadata.label: Absorption Color

metadata.help: Distance, in world space units, at which the Absorption Color is assumed to be reached. Can be used to control the rate of absorption.
metadata.label: Absorption Radius

metadata.help: Defines the roughness in the co-tangent direction, giving anisotropic reflection if the value differs from the roughness parameter value. A negative value (the default) means use the original roughness value, i.e. no anisotropy.
metadata.label: Anisotropy

metadata.help: Overrides the surface tangent as the anisotropy direction.
metadata.label: Direction

metadata.help: Rotates the anisotropy direction (possibly overridden by the previous attribute) around the normal, from 0 to 360 degrees.
metadata.label: Rotation

metadata.help: Switches the Fresnel between Artistic and Scientific parameters. The Artistic mode offers reflectivity in the normal direction and reflectivity90 at the grazing angle. The Scientific mode provides an index of refraction for each rgb component. Note that IOR is only used to calculate the reflectivity value used by the Schlick approximation. When reflectivity90 is 1, then both modes achieve identical results, as one can be mapped to the other.
metadata.label: Fresnel Mode

metadata.help: Shading normal, typically defined by bump or normal mapping. Defaults to the smooth surface normal if not set.
metadata.label: Normal

metadata.help: Color multiplier for external reflection. It should be used with parsimony, as a non-white value breaks physicality.
metadata.label: Reflection Tint

metadata.help: Color reflected by the surface in the normal direction.
metadata.label: Reflectivity

metadata.help: Color reflected by the surface at the grazing angle. Should be kept at 1,1,1 for most materials.
metadata.label: Reflectivity 90

metadata.help: The inverse of the exponent to use for Schlick.
metadata.label: ReflectivityProfile

metadata.help: Micro-facet distribution roughness.
metadata.label: Roughness

metadata.help: Anisotropy of the medium's phase function, ranging from full backward scattering at -1 to forward scattering at 1. Only takes effect if the Scatter Color is non-null.
metadata.label: Scatter Anisotropy

metadata.help: Color (aka scattering albedo) of the medium, defines what proportion of the light hitting volumetric particles is scattered around by the phase function (as opposed to just absorbed), for each color channel. Only takes effect if the corresponding channel has a non-null density, in other words if the Absorption Color value for that channel is inferior to 1.
metadata.label: Scatter Color

metadata.help: Color multiplier for rays going inside the medium (covers external transmission and internal reflection). It should be used sparingly, as a non-white value breaks physicality. The preferred way to define the color of a dielectric is through the Interior attributes right below.
metadata.label: Transmission Tint

metadata.help: Defines the amount of anisotropy, changing the co-tangent axis roughness from the original value to 1 (or to 0 with a negative value).
metadata.label: Anisotropy

metadata.help: Overrides the surface tangent as the anisotropy direction.
metadata.label: Direction

metadata.help: Rotates the anisotropy direction (possibly overridden by the previous attribute) around the normal, from 0 to 360 degrees.
metadata.label: Rotation

metadata.help: Thin film index of refraction, driving the iridescent effect.
metadata.label: IOR

metadata.help: Maximum thin film thickness in nanometers, driving the iridescent effect.
metadata.label: Max. Thickness

metadata.help: Minimum thin film thickness in nanometers, driving the iridescent effect.
metadata.label: Min. Thickness

metadata.help: Relative thin film thickness in the range from 0 to 1 will be scaled to the minimum and maximum thickness, respectively.
metadata.label: Relative Thickness

metadata.help: Micro-facet distribution roughness.
metadata.label: Roughness

metadata.help: Material used on the back side (as defined by the geometric normal).
metadata.label: Material Back

metadata.help: Material used on the front side (as defined by the geometric normal).
metadata.label: Material Front

metadata.help: Connect a mask here to apply a cutout pattern to your object. This is useful for cutouts like creating leaves and other thin, complex shapes. It can also be used as opacity for gray values for semi-opaque results when seen directly by the camera.
Unit is degree.
metadata.label: Longitude Offset

metadata.help: Color of the reflection from the diffuse and metallic base.
metadata.label: Base Color

metadata.help: Roughness of the diffuse reflection. Higher values cause the surface to appear flatter.
metadata.label: Base Diffuse Roughness

metadata.help: Specifies how metallic the base material appears (dials the base from pure dielectric to pure metal).
metadata.label: Base Metalness

metadata.help: Multiplier on the intensity of the reflection from the diffuse and metallic base.
metadata.label: Base Weight

metadata.help: The color of the clear-coat layer's transparency, due to absorption in the coat.
metadata.label: Coat Color

metadata.help: Modulates the physical coat darkening effect.
metadata.label: Coat Darkening

metadata.help: The index of refraction of the clear-coat layer.
metadata.label: Coat Index of Refraction

metadata.help: The roughness of the clear-coat reflections. The lower the value, the sharper the reflection.
metadata.label: Coat Roughness

metadata.help: The directional bias of the roughness of the clear-coat layer, resulting in increasingly stretched highlights along the coat tangent direction.
metadata.label: Coat Anisotropy

metadata.help: The presence weight of a reflective clear-coat layer on top of the material. Use for materials such as car paint or an oily layer.
metadata.label: Coat Weight

metadata.help: The color of the emitted light.
metadata.label: Emission Color

metadata.help: The amount of emitted light, as a luminance in nits.
metadata.label: Emission Luminance

metadata.help: The color of the fuzz layer.
metadata.label: Fuzz Color

metadata.help: The roughness of the fuzz layer.
metadata.label: Fuzz Roughness

metadata.help: The presence weight of a fuzz layer that can be used to approximate microfibers, for fabrics such as velvet and satin as well as dust grains.
metadata.label: Fuzz Weight

metadata.help: Input normal for coat layer
metadata.label: Coat Normal

metadata.help: Input geometric tangent for coat layer
metadata.label: Coat Tangent

metadata.help: Input geometric normal
metadata.label: Normal

metadata.help: The opacity of the entire material.
metadata.label: Opacity

metadata.help: Input geometric tangent
metadata.label: Tangent

metadata.help: If true the surface is double-sided and represents an infinitesimally thin shell. Suitable for extremely geometrically thin objects such as leaves or paper.
metadata.label: Thin Walled

metadata.help: Color of the specular reflection (controls the physical edge-tint for metals, and a non-physical overall tint for dielectrics).
metadata.label: Specular Color

metadata.help: Index of refraction of the dielectric base.
metadata.label: Specular Index of Refraction

metadata.help: The roughness of the specular reflection. Lower numbers produce sharper reflections, higher numbers produce blurrier reflections.
metadata.label: Specular Roughness

metadata.help: The directional bias of the roughness of the metal/dielectric base, resulting in increasingly stretched highlights along the tangent direction.
metadata.label: Specular Anisotropy

metadata.help: Multiplies the specular reflectivity.
metadata.label: Specular Weight

metadata.help: The observed reflection color of the subsurface scattering medium.
metadata.label: Subsurface Color

metadata.help: Length scale of the subsurface scattering mean free path.
metadata.label: Subsurface Radius

metadata.help: RGB multiplier to subsurface_radius, giving the per-channel scattering mean-free-paths.
metadata.label: Subsurface Radius Scale

metadata.help: Controls the phase-function of subsurface scattering, where zero scatters light evenly, positive values scatter forwards, and negative values scatter backwards.
metadata.label: Subsurface Anisotropy

metadata.help: Mixture weight which dials the opaque dielectric base between diffuse reflection and subsurface scattering. A value of 1.0 indicates full subsurface scattering and a value 0 for diffuse reflection only.
metadata.label: Subsurface Weight

metadata.help: The index of refraction of the thin-film.
metadata.label: Thin Film Index of Refraction

metadata.help: The thickness of the thin-film layer on the base (in micrometers).
metadata.label: Thin Film Thickness

metadata.help: Coverage weight of the thin-film. Use for materials such as multi-tone car paint or soap bubbles.
metadata.label: Thin Film Weight

metadata.help: Controls color of the transparent base due to Beer's law volumetric absorption under the surface (reverts to a non-physical tint when transmission_depth is zero).
metadata.label: Transmission Color

metadata.help: Specifies the distance light travels inside the transparent base before it becomes exactly the transmission_color according to Beer's law.
metadata.label: Transmission Depth

metadata.help: Physical Abbe number of the dielectric medium, describing how much the dielectric index of refraction varies across wavelengths.
metadata.label: Transmission Dispersion Abbe Number

metadata.help: Linearly scales the amount of dispersion.
metadata.label: Transmission Dispersion Scale

metadata.help: Controls the color of light volumetrically scattered inside the transparent base. Suitable for materials with visually significant scattering such as honey, fruit juice, murky water, opalescent glass, or milky glass.
metadata.label: Transmission Scatter

metadata.help: The amount of directional bias, or anisotropy, of the volumetric scattering in the transparent base.
metadata.label: Transmission Anisotropy

metadata.help: Mixture weight between the transparent and opaque dielectric base. The greater the value the more transparent the material.
metadata.label: Transmission Weight
